Front: How do you design a system for high availability across multiple regions?
Back: You deploy services across regions with replication and failover so traffic can shift when one region fails, preventing full outages.
Why: Regional outages are real, and without redundancy all users lose access.
When: Use for global or mission-critical systems.
Tradeoffs: Improves resilience and latency for global users but increases cost, coordination, and consistency complexity.
Trap: Mistake: assuming one region is enough. Why wrong: regions can fail entirely. Consequence: total system outage.
Scenario: A SaaS app fails over traffic to another region during an outage.

Front: What is a single point of failure and how do you remove it?
Back: A single point of failure is any component whose failure stops the system; removing it requires redundancy and automated failover.
Why: It matters because one weak component can take down an otherwise distributed system.
Tradeoffs: Improves uptime but increases cost and operational complexity.
Trap: Mistake: missing hidden dependencies. Why wrong: unnoticed SPOFs remain. Consequence: unexpected outages.
Scenario: A single database instance causes downtime when it crashes.

Front: What is the circuit breaker pattern and why is it important?
Back: A circuit breaker stops calls to failing services after a threshold, preventing cascading failures and giving the dependency time to recover.
Why: It matters because repeated failures can overload both caller and dependency.
Tradeoffs: Improves stability but may temporarily reject valid requests.
Trap: Mistake: infinite retries. Why wrong: amplifies load on failing service. Consequence: cascading outage.
Scenario: API stops calling failing payment service.

Front: What is failover and how should it be designed?
Back: Failover switches traffic to a backup system when the primary fails, ensuring continuity if designed and tested properly.
Why: It matters because redundancy alone is useless without working failover.
Tradeoffs: Improves availability but adds complexity and testing requirements.
Trap: Mistake: untested failover. Why wrong: hidden gaps appear during failure. Consequence: downtime.
Scenario: Replica database takes over when primary fails.

Front: What is eventual consistency and when is it acceptable?
Back: Eventual consistency allows temporary differences between replicas so systems remain available and scale, with data converging later.
Why: It matters because strict consistency limits scalability.
When: Use for non-critical or read-heavy data.
Tradeoffs: Improves availability but risks stale reads.
Trap: Mistake: using for critical data. Why wrong: delays can break correctness. Consequence: inconsistent user experience.
Scenario: Social feeds tolerate delayed updates.

Front: What is strong consistency and when is it required?
Back: Strong consistency ensures every read reflects the latest write, guaranteeing correctness across nodes.
Why: It matters because some systems cannot tolerate stale data.
When: Use in financial or critical workflows.
Tradeoffs: Improves correctness but reduces availability and increases latency.
Trap: Mistake: using everywhere. Why wrong: unnecessary coordination cost. Consequence: reduced scalability.
Scenario: Bank balances must always be accurate.

Front: What is request hedging and how does it reduce latency?
Back: Request hedging sends duplicate requests and uses the fastest response, reducing tail latency from slow instances.
Why: It matters because outliers dominate user experience.
Tradeoffs: Improves responsiveness but increases resource usage.
Trap: Mistake: overusing hedging. Why wrong: duplicates increase load. Consequence: wasted capacity.
Scenario: Search requests sent to multiple replicas.

Front: What is a load shedding strategy?
Back: Load shedding drops excess or low-priority requests when capacity is exceeded to protect system stability.
Why: It matters because accepting all traffic can crash the system.
Tradeoffs: Maintains stability but sacrifices some requests.
Trap: Mistake: processing all traffic. Why wrong: system overload. Consequence: full outage.
Scenario: Dropping low-priority requests during spikes.

Front: What is a CDN and how does it improve performance?
Back: A CDN caches content at edge locations near users, reducing latency and offloading origin servers.
Why: It matters because distance and load increase response time.
When: Use for static and cacheable content.
Tradeoffs: Improves speed but adds cache invalidation complexity.
Trap: Mistake: caching dynamic data incorrectly. Why wrong: stale responses. Consequence: incorrect content.
Scenario: Images served from edge nodes.

Front: What is request batching and when should you use it?
Back: Batching combines multiple requests into one to reduce overhead and improve throughput.
Why: It matters because network and processing overhead accumulate.
Tradeoffs: Improves efficiency but increases per-request latency.
Trap: Mistake: batching everything. Why wrong: delays responses. Consequence: slower user experience.
Scenario: Batching database writes improves throughput.

Front: What is idempotency and why is it critical in distributed systems?
Back: Idempotency ensures repeated requests produce the same result, enabling safe retries without side effects.
Why: It matters because retries are common in unreliable networks.
Tradeoffs: Improves reliability but requires careful design.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring idempotency. Why wrong: duplicate effects occur. Consequence: inconsistent state.
Scenario: Duplicate payment requests must not double charge.

Front: What is a hot spot in distributed systems?
Back: A hot spot occurs when uneven traffic concentrates on one node or partition, creating a bottleneck.
Why: It matters because uneven load limits scalability.
Tradeoffs: Improves scaling but requires careful key design.
Trap: Mistake: poor partitioning key. Why wrong: uneven load. Consequence: bottlenecks.
Scenario: All traffic routed to one shard.

Front: What is service discovery and why is it needed?
Back: Service discovery allows dynamic lookup of service instances so systems can scale without hardcoded endpoints.
Why: It matters because static configurations break in dynamic environments.
Tradeoffs: Improves flexibility but adds infrastructure complexity.
Trap: Mistake: hardcoding endpoints. Why wrong: breaks scaling. Consequence: brittle system.
Scenario: Using service registry like Consul.

Front: What is a retry strategy and how should it be implemented?
Back: Retries reattempt failed requests with controlled backoff to handle transient failures without overwhelming systems.
Why: It matters because naive retries amplify failures.
Tradeoffs: Improves resilience but can increase load.
Trap: Mistake: aggressive retries. Why wrong: amplifies failure. Consequence: cascading issues.
Scenario: Retry API calls with delay.

Front: What is exponential backoff and why is it important?
Back: Exponential backoff increases retry delays to reduce pressure on failing systems.
Why: It matters because immediate retries create retry storms.
Tradeoffs: Improves stability but increases latency.
Trap: Mistake: constant retries. Why wrong: overload. Consequence: system collapse.
Scenario: Retry delays grow over time.

Front: What is a fan-out pattern and what risks does it introduce?
Back: Fan-out sends requests to multiple services in parallel, improving speed but increasing dependency risk.
Why: It matters because more dependencies increase failure probability.
Tradeoffs: Improves latency but increases failure surface.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring partial failures. Why wrong: one failure blocks result. Consequence: degraded reliability.
Scenario: Aggregating data from multiple services.

Front: What is bulkheading and how does it improve resilience?
Back: Bulkheading isolates resources so failures in one component do not affect others.
Why: It matters because shared resources create cascading failures.
Tradeoffs: Improves isolation but increases complexity.
Trap: Mistake: shared resources. Why wrong: failures spread. Consequence: widespread outage.
Scenario: Separate thread pools per service.

Front: What is throttling and how is it different from rate limiting?
Back: Throttling dynamically adjusts traffic based on system health, while rate limiting enforces fixed limits.
Why: It matters because static limits cannot adapt to real-time conditions.
Tradeoffs: Improves flexibility but adds complexity.
Trap: Mistake: treating them as same. Why wrong: different behaviors. Consequence: poor protection.
Scenario: Reduce traffic when CPU spikes.

Front: What is a distributed cache and when should you use it?
Back: A distributed cache shares cached data across nodes, improving scalability and consistency across instances.
Why: It matters because local caches cause inconsistencies.
Tradeoffs: Improves performance but adds coordination overhead.
Trap: Mistake: local-only cache. Why wrong: inconsistent data. Consequence: cache misses.
Scenario: Redis cluster used for caching.

Front: What is data partitioning and why is it important?
Back: Partitioning splits data across nodes to distribute load and improve scalability.
Why: It matters because single-node limits restrict growth.
Tradeoffs: Improves scale but complicates queries.
Trap: Mistake: poor partition strategy. Why wrong: uneven load. Consequence: bottlenecks.
Scenario: Sharding users across databases.

Front: What is queue-based buffering and why does it help under bursty load?
Back: Queue-based buffering decouples producers from consumers so incoming spikes are absorbed and processed later, preventing immediate overload of downstream systems.
Why: It matters because burst traffic can overwhelm dependencies even if average load is safe.
When: Use for async, bursty, or delay-tolerant workloads.
Tradeoffs: Improves resilience and smoothing but introduces latency, backlog risk, and eventual consistency.
Trap: Mistake: treating queues as infinite buffers. Why wrong: if processing < arrival rate, backlog grows unbounded. Consequence: delays, timeouts, or dropped work.
Scenario: Order service accepts requests instantly and processes emails asynchronously.

Front: What is a backlog and how do you reason about it in system design?
Back: A backlog is queued work waiting to be processed, representing how far the system is behind real-time demand.
Why: It matters because backlog growth signals throughput issues before failures appear.
When: Use in queues, worker systems, and pipelines.
Tradeoffs: Helps capacity planning but can hide issues if traffic temporarily drops.
Trap: Mistake: only monitoring success rates. Why wrong: system can be falling behind silently. Consequence: delayed results surface later.
Scenario: Video processing jobs pile up while workers lag behind.

Front: What is active-active versus active-passive redundancy?
Back: Active-active serves traffic from multiple live nodes simultaneously, while active-passive keeps standby systems for failover.
Why: It matters because the model determines failover speed and complexity.
When: Use active-active for low-latency global systems, active-passive for simpler recovery.
Tradeoffs: Active-active improves availability and utilization but complicates consistency; active-passive is simpler but slower to recover.
Trap: Mistake: using active-active without managing state consistency. Why wrong: concurrent updates conflict. Consequence: data inconsistency or failover issues.
Scenario: Global apps use active-active, payment systems often use active-passive.

Front: What is quorum and when is it useful in highly available systems?
Back: Quorum requires a majority of nodes to agree before accepting operations, preventing decisions from isolated or outdated nodes.
Why: It matters because it balances consistency with fault tolerance.
When: Use in replicated databases and consensus systems.
Tradeoffs: Improves correctness but increases latency and can reduce availability.
Trap: Mistake: assuming more nodes means always more availability. Why wrong: quorum still needs majority. Consequence: system stalls under partial failure.
Scenario: Database requires majority write acknowledgments.

Front: What is consensus and why is it harder than simple replication?
Back: Consensus ensures nodes agree on a single authoritative order of operations, even under failure, not just copying data.
Why: It matters because coordination is required for correctness in distributed systems.
When: Use for leader election and strongly consistent systems.
Tradeoffs: Ensures correctness but adds latency and complexity.
Trap: Mistake: treating replication as consensus. Why wrong: replication alone does not resolve conflicts. Consequence: inconsistent system state.
Scenario: Raft ensures only one leader writes changes.

Front: What is graceful degradation versus fail-fast behavior?
Back: Graceful degradation keeps partial functionality under stress, while fail-fast rejects work early to avoid corruption or overload.
Why: It matters because different failures require different responses.
When: Use degradation for optional features, fail-fast for critical correctness.
Tradeoffs: Degradation preserves usability but reduces quality; fail-fast protects integrity but disrupts users.
Trap: Mistake: applying one pattern universally. Why wrong: not all failures are equal. Consequence: either unnecessary outages or hidden errors.
Scenario: Media site drops recommendations, payment service rejects unsafe requests.

Front: How does autoscaling help, and what problems does it not solve?
Back: Autoscaling adjusts capacity based on load, helping absorb demand changes but not fixing bottlenecks or slow dependencies.
Why: It matters because scaling compute alone does not fix system design flaws.
When: Use for stateless workloads with clear metrics.
Tradeoffs: Improves elasticity but cannot solve shared resource limits.
Trap: Mistake: assuming autoscaling solves all scaling issues. Why wrong: bottlenecks remain. Consequence: failures persist despite scaling.
Scenario: API scales but database still limits throughput.

Front: What is load balancing health checking and why can bad health checks cause outages?
Back: Health checks determine which nodes receive traffic, so incorrect checks can route traffic to failing nodes or remove healthy ones.
Why: It matters because routing decisions directly impact availability.
When: Use in load balancers and service meshes.
Tradeoffs: Fast detection improves failover but overly strict checks cause instability.
Trap: Mistake: shallow or overly strict checks. Why wrong: misclassifies nodes. Consequence: traffic routed incorrectly.
Scenario: Service reports healthy despite DB outage.

Front: What is tail drop versus priority shedding in overload control?
Back: Tail drop rejects requests after capacity is full, while priority shedding drops lower-value work first to protect critical operations.
Why: It matters because not all traffic has equal value.
When: Use priority shedding for mixed workloads.
Tradeoffs: Tail drop is simple but blunt; priority shedding is smarter but complex.
Trap: Mistake: treating all traffic equally. Why wrong: critical work gets dropped. Consequence: business impact.
Scenario: Checkout requests preserved while analytics dropped.

Front: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous workflows in system design?
Back: Synchronous workflows block until completion, while asynchronous workflows defer processing to background systems.
Why: It matters because this choice impacts latency, reliability, and scalability.
When: Use sync for immediate results, async for long-running tasks.
Tradeoffs: Sync is simpler but fragile; async improves resilience but adds complexity.
Trap: Mistake: making everything synchronous. Why wrong: long tasks block resources. Consequence: timeouts and poor performance.
Scenario: Video upload is async, validation is sync.

Front: What is head-of-line blocking and where does it show up in system design?
Back: Head-of-line blocking occurs when a slow request delays all subsequent work in a shared queue or resource.
Why: It matters because it increases latency even when capacity exists.
When: Use isolation or prioritization to prevent it.
Tradeoffs: Shared queues are simple but can create unfair delays.
Trap: Mistake: mixing workloads. Why wrong: slow tasks block fast ones. Consequence: latency spikes.
Scenario: Heavy jobs block lightweight requests.

Front: What is a bottleneck, and why does optimizing the wrong layer waste effort?
Back: A bottleneck is the component limiting system throughput, so optimizing non-limiting parts has little impact.
Why: It matters because effort must target the true constraint.
Tradeoffs: Fixing bottlenecks improves performance but may shift them elsewhere.
Trap: Mistake: optimizing visible components. Why wrong: not actual constraint. Consequence: no improvement.
Scenario: Scaling servers doesn’t help if DB is bottleneck.

Front: What is redundancy at the network edge, and why is it different from server redundancy?
Back: Edge redundancy duplicates ingress points like DNS and load balancers, ensuring users can reach the system even if entry paths fail.
Why: It matters because compute redundancy is useless if traffic cannot reach it.
Tradeoffs: Improves availability but adds network complexity.
Trap: Mistake: only adding server redundancy. Why wrong: ingress can still fail. Consequence: full outage.
Scenario: Single load balancer failure blocks access.

Front: What is read-heavy versus write-heavy design pressure, and why does it matter for caching and scaling?
Back: Read-heavy systems optimize for fast retrieval, while write-heavy systems optimize for ingestion and durability, requiring different architectures.
Why: It matters because optimizations differ drastically.
When: Use caching for reads, buffering for writes.
Tradeoffs: Read optimizations hurt writes and vice versa.
Trap: Mistake: applying read strategies to write-heavy systems. Why wrong: doesn’t address bottleneck. Consequence: poor performance.
Scenario: Caching helps feeds, not ingestion pipelines.

Front: What is request coalescing and why does it help under load?
Back: Request coalescing merges identical in-flight requests so only one backend call is made, reducing duplicate work.
Why: It matters because hot keys can overwhelm systems.
When: Use for caching and expensive reads.
Tradeoffs: Improves efficiency but adds coordination complexity.
Trap: Mistake: allowing duplicate work. Why wrong: multiplies load. Consequence: system overload.
Scenario: Many users request same uncached resource.

Front: What is a rate limit budget and why is it better than a vague 'protect the API' goal?
Back: A rate limit budget defines explicit limits tied to system capacity, ensuring fair and predictable usage.
Why: It matters because vague limits fail to protect systems effectively.
Tradeoffs: Improves fairness but requires tuning.
Trap: Mistake: arbitrary limits. Why wrong: not aligned with capacity. Consequence: overload or blocked users.
Scenario: API assigns per-tenant request quotas.

Front: What is a retry storm and how do you prevent it?
Back: A retry storm occurs when many clients retry simultaneously, overwhelming a failing system and delaying recovery.
Why: It matters because retries can amplify failures.
Tradeoffs: Improves resilience but adds latency.
Trap: Mistake: immediate retries. Why wrong: synchronized load spike. Consequence: extended outage.
Scenario: Clients retry failed requests instantly.

Front: What is overprovisioning, and when is it a rational design choice?
Back: Overprovisioning keeps extra capacity available to handle spikes and failures without relying solely on scaling.
Why: It matters because scaling may not react fast enough.
Tradeoffs: Improves resilience but increases cost.
Trap: Mistake: optimizing only for average load. Why wrong: ignores spikes. Consequence: failures under peak.
Scenario: Login service maintains spare capacity.

Front: What is dependency isolation, and why does it matter more as systems grow?
Back: Dependency isolation separates resources so failures in one dependency do not consume all capacity.
Why: It matters because shared resources amplify failures.
Tradeoffs: Improves resilience but adds complexity.
Trap: Mistake: shared resource pools. Why wrong: one failure spreads. Consequence: system-wide outage.
Scenario: Separate pools for payment vs recommendations.

Front: What is availability budget thinking, and how does it change system design choices?
Back: Availability budgets define acceptable downtime targets, guiding how much redundancy and complexity to invest in.
Why: It matters because vague goals lead to inconsistent design decisions.
Tradeoffs: Improves prioritization but increases cost at higher targets.
Trap: Mistake: vague availability goals. Why wrong: unclear tradeoffs. Consequence: over/under engineering.
Scenario: Critical systems require higher availability targets.
